attractions.name: Alki Beach Park
attractions.description: Alki Beach Park is a 135.9-acre park located in the West Seattle neighborhood of Seattle, Washington. It consists of the Elliott Bay beach between Alki Point and Duwamish Head, featuring a 0.5-mile beachfront that was the first public salt-water bathing beach on the west coast of the United States. The park includes a replica Statue of Liberty installed in 1952, the Alki Point Monument, and is operated by Seattle Parks and Recreation.
attractions.hours: 4:00 AM – 11:30 PM daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: First public salt-water bathing beach on the West Coast of the United States
Replica of the Statue of Liberty installed in 1952
Half-mile of beachfront along Elliott Bay
Alki Point Monument memorializing Seattle's pioneer White settlers
Dogs allowed in the park (not on the beach)
135.9-acre park with views of the Olympic Mountains
attractions.tips: Dogs are allowed in the park but not on the beach — there is a $500 fine for pets on the beach. The park is open from 4am to 11:30pm daily. Parking can be limited during peak summer months, so arrive early. The beach offers stunning sunset views of the Olympic Mountains across Elliott Bay.
attractions.nearby: West Seattle Bridge, Alki Point Lighthouse, Lincoln Park, Elliott Bay Trail, West Seattle Farmers Market, Duwamish Head Park

attractions.name: Bill Speidel's Underground Tour
attractions.description: Bill Speidel's Underground Tour is Seattle's most unusual attraction, offering a 75-minute guided walking tour through the buried Victorian-era passageways beneath Pioneer Square. These underground storefronts and sidewalks were sealed when the city rebuilt on top of itself after the Great Fire of 1889 and the subsequent street-raising project. The tour combines Seattle history with humorous storytelling, taking visitors through the world-famous Seattle Underground's interconnecting tunnels.
attractions.hours: April–September: 9:00 AM – 7:00 PM (extra tours every half hour June–August, 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM, box office only); October–March: 10:00 AM – 6:00 PM; December 24: 10:00 AM – 1:00 PM; December 26–31: 9:00 AM – 6:00 PM
attractions.days_open: Daily, year-round (Closed Thanksgiving Day and Christmas Day)
attractions.admission: Adults ~$26; Seniors/Students ~$20; Children (7–12) ~$13; Children under 7: Free. 15.35% sales/admission tax added at checkout.
highlights: World's only guided tour through the buried Victorian-era passageways of Seattle's Underground
75-minute humorous walking tour blending history with storytelling
Tours begin beneath Doc Maynard's Public House, a restored 1890s saloon
Explores multiple underground levels including buried storefronts and sidewalks
Daily tours year-round in historic Pioneer Square, Seattle's birthplace
The original Underground Tour operator, founded by Bill Speidel in 1965
attractions.tips: Book tickets in advance online or by phone, especially for summer and weekend tours which sell out. Arrive early if purchasing walk-up tickets. Wear comfortable shoes as the tour involves stairs, uneven surfaces, and tight corridors. Children under 7 are free but may find the 75-minute tour challenging. The tour is not wheelchair accessible due to stairs and narrow passageways.
attractions.nearby: Smith Tower (Seattle's first skyscraper), Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park, Pioneer Square Historic District, Pike Place Market (12–15 min walk), Seattle Great Wheel, The Great Northern Tunnel, Columbia Center, Waterfront Seattle

attractions.name: Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture
attractions.description: The Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture is the natural history museum of the University of Washington in Seattle, Washington. It is the oldest museum in Washington state, established in 1899, and boasts a collection of more than 16 million artifacts including the world's largest collection of spread bird wings. The museum features working labs, dinosaur fossils, Northwest Native art, and cultural pieces from across the globe.
attractions.hours: Tuesday – Sunday: 10 AM – 5 PM (Last admission at 4:30 PM)
attractions.admission: General Admission: $24; Senior (62+): $22; Youth (4-14): $16; Child (3 & under): FREE; Non-UW Student (with ID): $16; Member Adult/Youth: FREE; UW Student: FREE; UW Faculty & Staff: FREE; Reciprocal Members: FREE; Museums For All: FREE
highlights: Working labs visible to the public with scientists at work
World's largest collection of spread bird wings (over 16 million total artifacts)
Dinosaur fossils and T. rex exhibits
Northwest Native art and cultural collections
Smithsonian Affiliate museum
Located on the University of Washington campus
attractions.tips: Book timed tickets online in advance to skip lines, especially on weekends. Free First Thursdays offer complimentary admission. Arrive at any time during your reserved hour slot. Allow 2-3 hours for a full visit. Free First Thursdays may be crowded.
attractions.nearby: University of Washington campus, Suzzallo Library, UW Drumheller Fountain, University Village shopping district, Green Lake Park, Friedhelm & Jori Okamoto Garden

attractions.name: Capitol Hill
attractions.description: Capitol Hill is one of Seattle's most vibrant and diverse neighborhoods, known for its lively nightlife, LGBTQ+ culture, arts scene, and eclectic dining. Located just east of downtown, it features a mix of historic homes, trendy boutiques, music venues, and parks. The area is a major cultural hub and one of Seattle's most densely populated neighborhoods.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (neighborhood/public area)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Vibrant LGBTQ+ community and culture
Diverse nightlife and live music venues
Eclectic dining and food scene
Historic architecture and tree-lined streets
Cal Anderson Park
Broadway shopping and entertainment corridor
attractions.tips: Capitol Hill is best explored on foot or by public transit. Visit during the day for shopping and cafes, and return in the evening for nightlife. Cal Anderson Park is great for a picnic. The neighborhood is very walkable from downtown Seattle.
attractions.nearby: Pike Place Market, Seattle Art Museum, Volunteer Park, Seattle Central College, South Lake Union

attractions.name: Chihuly Garden and Glass
attractions.description: Chihuly Garden and Glass is an exhibit in the Seattle Center directly next to the Space Needle, showcasing the studio glass of artist Dale Chihuly. It opened in May 2012 and features three primary components: the Garden, the Glasshouse, and the Interior Exhibits, along with live glass demonstrations, a theater, and a bar. The 100-foot-long suspended sculpture inside the Glasshouse is one of Chihuly's largest installations.
attractions.hours: Saturday: 9:00AM – 5:00PM; Sunday: 9:30AM – 9:00PM; Monday: 10:00AM – 9:00PM; Tuesday: 10:00AM – 9:00PM; Wednesday: 10:00AM – 9:00PM; Thursday: 10:00AM – 9:00PM; Friday: 9:30AM – 9:30PM
attractions.admission: General Admission: $35 – $42.50; Senior: $30 – $37.50; Youth (5-12): $30 – $37.50; Children (under 4): Free; Admission + Space Needle: $69; CityPASS: $119 – $139
highlights: The Glasshouse with a 100-foot-long suspended glass sculpture featuring vibrant red, yellow, and orange palette
Eight indoor galleries showcasing over 40 years of Chihuly's glass art experimentation
Outdoor Garden with site-specific installations complemented by lush landscaping
Live glassblowing demonstrations at the Community Hot Shop daily beginning at 11am
Persian Ceiling and Mille Fiori rooms featuring large-scale architectural glass installations
Theater showing short films about Chihuly's working process and project installations
attractions.tips: Plan for 1-2 hours to see the Galleries, Garden, and Glasshouse. Free gallery talks and garden tours are offered throughout the day beginning at 10:30am. Photos are encouraged with flash permitted, but no selfie sticks or tripods. The Glasshouse is sometimes reserved for private events, so check ahead. Complimentary coat check is available for large strollers, bags, and suitcases. Only service animals are allowed. Visit early morning to avoid crowds.
attractions.nearby: Space Needle, Pacific Science Center, Museum of Pop Culture (MoPOP), Seattle Center Monorail, Climate Pledge Arena

attractions.name: Chinatown–International District
attractions.description: The Chinatown–International District (CID) is one of the nation's most vital Asian American districts in Seattle, Washington. This vibrant neighborhood encompasses Chinatown, Japantown, Filipinotown, and Little Saigon, celebrating a rich tapestry of Asian cultures and traditions. Visitors can explore authentic cuisine, historic landmarks like the Historic Chinatown Gate, the Wing Luke Museum, and unique Asian-owned shops and galleries. The area is a designated National Historic Place and serves as a major cultural hub and tourist attraction in downtown Seattle.
attractions.address: Bounded by 4th Avenue South (west) to Rainier Avenue (east), and Yesler Way (north) to Charles Street/Dearborn (south)
attractions.hours: The neighborhood is open 24/7 as a public district. Individual businesses have varying hours. Notable venues: Wing Luke Museum: 10am-5pm (Tue-Sat); Uwajimaya: 8am-10pm daily; Seattle Pinball Museum: 12pm-8pm (Sun-Thu), 12pm-10pm (Fri-Sat); International District/Chinatown Community Center: Mon/Wed/Fri 11am-9pm, Tue/Thu 9:30am-6pm, Sat 9am-5pm, Sun closed; Seattle Public Library ID Branch: Mon-Thu & Sat-Sun 10am-6pm, Fri closed
attractions.days_open: Open daily (neighborhood itself is publicly accessible 24/7)
attractions.admission: Free (neighborhood access is free; individual attractions may have admission fees)
highlights: Historic Chinatown Gate (Panama Gate) - a 45-foot tall Paifang archway and iconic neighborhood entrance
Wing Luke Museum of the Asian Pacific American Experience - Smithsonian-affiliated museum
Hing Hay Park - historic park with Dragon Garden and community events
Uwajimaya - legendary Asian supermarket with imported goods and food court
Lunar New Year Festival and Dragon Fest - annual pan-Asian cultural celebrations
Little Saigon, Chinatown, and Japantown - three distinct cultural enclaves within the district
attractions.tips: The neighborhood is a major public transit hub via the International District/Chinatown Link light rail station. Visit during Lunar New Year (January/February) or Dragon Fest (summer) for vibrant cultural celebrations. Many restaurants offer dim sum - Jade Garden is a popular choice. Park at the nearby Union Station garage or use public transit. The area can be busy on weekends, especially around Uwajimaya. Some businesses close early or on different days, so check ahead. The Light Rail connects directly to SeaTac Airport for easy access.
attractions.nearby: Pioneer Square Historic District, King Street Station, Seattle Central Library, Seattle Great Wheel, Pike Place Market, Union Station, Smith Tower, Seattle Art Museum, Occidental Park

attractions.name: Discovery Park
attractions.description: Discovery Park is a 534-acre urban park on the shores of Puget Sound in the Magnolia neighborhood of Seattle. As the city's largest public park, it contains 11.81 miles of walking trails including the Discovery Park Loop Trail, a National Recreation Trail. The park features forests, beaches, prairies, and bluffs, and is home to the West Point Lighthouse and the historic Fort Lawton district.
attractions.hours: 4:00 AM – 11:30 PM daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: West Point Lighthouse – the westernmost point of Seattle
11.81 miles of walking trails including the 2.8-mile Discovery Park Loop Trail
534 acres of diverse landscapes: forests, beaches, prairies, and bluffs
Historic Fort Lawton district and Daybreak Star Cultural Center
Excellent birdwatching with 270 recorded species per Seattle Audubon Society
Views of Elliott and Shilshole Bays, Olympic Mountains, and Mount Rainier
attractions.tips: Wear sturdy shoes for the Loop Trail which includes forest, meadow, and beach sections. The park is excellent for birdwatching – bring binoculars. Visit during early morning or late afternoon for best wildlife viewing and fewer crowds. The beach areas can be slippery; be cautious on coastal trails. Parking is available at the main entrance near the Environmental Learning Center.
attractions.nearby: Magnolia neighborhood shops and restaurants, Olympic Sculpture Park, Seattle Center/Space Needle, Pike Place Market, Kerry Park viewpoint

attractions.name: Fremont Solstice Parade
attractions.description: The Fremont Solstice Parade is an annual, unjuried community parade held each June in the Fremont neighborhood of Seattle, Washington. Founded in 1989 by Barbara Luecke and Peter Toms, the parade celebrates the summer solstice with body-painted cyclists, handmade floats, and artistic performances. It distinguishes itself from other parades with rules banning printed words, logos, motorized vehicles, live animals, and functional weapons, emphasizing creativity and community spirit.
attractions.hours: Not applicable — single-day event held annually on a Saturday in mid-to-late June (typically around the summer solstice, June 20-21)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Body-painted cyclists riding down N 36th Street
Handmade artistic floats and community-built creations
Unjuried and non-commercial — no printed words, logos, or signs allowed
Live music and street performances
Family-friendly community celebration of the summer solstice
Held annually since 1989 in Seattle's quirky Fremont neighborhood
attractions.tips: Arrive early to secure a good viewing spot along N 36th Street between Phinney Avenue and Evanston Avenue. Bring sunscreen, water, and a hat as it's an outdoor summer event. The parade is typically followed by the Fremont Fair weekend with arts, crafts, and food vendors. Parking is limited — consider public transit or rideshare. The event draws large crowds, so expect congestion in the area.
attractions.nearby: Fremont Troll, Fremont Sunday Market, Burke-Gilman Trail, Gas Works Park, Fremont Brewing, Theo Chocolate Factory

attractions.name: Fremont Troll
attractions.description: The Fremont Troll, also known as The Troll or the Troll Under the Bridge, is a public sculpture in Fremont, Seattle, Washington. It is a colossal mixed media statue located under the north end of the George Washington Memorial Bridge (Aurora Bridge), depicting a troll clutching an actual Volkswagen Beetle. The sculpture was created in 1990 by four artists—Steve Badanes, Will Martin, Donna Walter, and Ross Whitehead—and has become an iconic Seattle landmark.
attractions.address: N 36th St at Troll Ave N, under the Aurora Bridge
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Colossal 18 ft (5.5 m) tall troll sculpture made of steel rebar, wire, and concrete
Troll is clutching a real Volkswagen Beetle with a California license plate
Located under the iconic Aurora Bridge (George Washington Memorial Bridge)
Created in 1990 by four artists drawing from Scandinavian folklore
One of Seattle's most photographed and recognizable landmarks
Owned by the City of Seattle
attractions.tips: The troll is located in a small parking area under the bridge—parking can be limited during peak tourist times. The best time to visit is early morning or late evening to avoid crowds. The sculpture is 18 feet tall and makes for excellent photo opportunities. It's a quick stop that pairs well with exploring the broader Fremont neighborhood.
attractions.nearby: Fremont Sunday Market, Burke-Gilman Trail, Fremont Brewing, Gas Works Park, Theo Chocolate, Fremont Abbey Arts Center, Fremont Rocket, Lenin Statue

attractions.name: Frye Art Museum
attractions.description: The Frye Art Museum is a modern and contemporary art museum in Seattle's First Hill neighborhood. Founded in 1952 to house the private collection of Charles and Emma Frye, it features rotating temporary exhibitions of emerging and contemporary artists alongside its permanent collection. The museum is housed in a stunning Olson Sundberg Kundig-designed building and is notable for being free to the public every day.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 11 am–5 pm; Thursday: 11 am–8 pm; Closed Monday–Tuesday. Holiday closures: Independence Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas Day, New Year's Day. Closes at 3 pm on Christmas Eve and New Year's Eve.
attractions.admission: Free — admission is always free
highlights: Free admission every day of operation
Olson Sundberg Kundig-designed building in historic First Hill
Permanent collection of 19th-century to contemporary painting and sculpture
Rotating exhibitions of emerging and global artists
MariPili at Café Frieda — Galician-inspired café and happy hour
Museum Store featuring local makers and exhibition materials
attractions.tips: Admission is always free. Free 45-minute guided gallery conversations are offered Fridays at 1 pm. Handheld photography is allowed in most areas. The café offers Thursday happy hour specials from 5–7:30 pm. Paid street parking is available along nearby streets, or book ahead with SpotHero. There are two bike racks near the museum.
attractions.nearby: Seattle Art Museum, Pioneer Square, Seattle Central Library, Washington State Convention Center, Columbia City, First Hill Streetcar

attractions.name: Gas Works Park
attractions.description: Gas Works Park is a 19.1-acre public park on the shores of Lake Union in Seattle, built on the site of the former Seattle Gas Light Company gasification plant. The park preserves much of the old industrial gasification equipment as artistic structures, making it a unique example of industrial archaeology. It features a large play barn, kite-flying hill, and offers panoramic views of downtown Seattle and the lake.
attractions.hours: 6 a.m. - 10 p.m. daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Preserved industrial gasification plant structures from the early 20th century
Large play barn and play area for children
Big hill popular for kite flying
Panoramic views of Lake Union and downtown Seattle skyline
Access to the Burke-Gilman Trail for walking, running, and biking
Picnic areas with grills and reservable spaces
attractions.tips: Arrive early on sunny weekends as parking fills up quickly. The park is popular for picnics, kite flying, and watching fireworks on July 4th. Access to Lake Union is restricted due to hazardous substances in the lake sediment — do not swim. Bring sunscreen as there is limited shade on the main hill.
attractions.nearby: Burke-Gilman Trail, University of Washington campus, UW Medical Center, Wallingford neighborhood, Fremont neighborhood, Lake Union Park

attractions.name: Georgetown, Seattle
attractions.description: Georgetown is a neighborhood in southern Seattle, Washington, and is arguably the city's oldest neighborhood, founded on September 27, 1851. It is bounded by the BNSF and Union Pacific railroads to the north, the Duwamish River to the west, Interstate 5 to the east, and Boeing Field to the south. Despite being surrounded by industry and major transportation corridors, Georgetown retains a sizeable number of residences, businesses, and a distinctive artistic character.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (neighborhood/public area)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Historic Georgetown sign — iconic neon-lit mural on a brick building
Oldest neighborhood in Seattle, founded in 1851
Vibrant arts scene with numerous galleries, studios, and murals
Historic Stock House of the Georgetown Brewery
Proximity to Boeing Field (King County International Airport)
Industrial-chic character with breweries, cafes, and creative businesses
attractions.tips: Georgetown is best explored on foot or by bike to discover its hidden art installations, murals, and small businesses. Visit during the evening to see the iconic Georgetown sign lit up. The First Thursday Art Walk is a popular monthly event. Parking can be limited in some areas.
attractions.nearby: Beacon Hill, South Park, SODO District, Boeing Field, Duwamish River, West Seattle

attractions.name: Green Lake Park
attractions.description: Green Lake Park is one of Seattle's most beloved parks, featuring a 259-acre lake surrounded by a 2.8-mile paved car-free recreational path. The park offers swimming beaches, sports fields, a community center, boating access, and lush green spaces in the heart of the city. It is a premier destination for walking, running, cycling, and water activities.
attractions.address: 7201 East Green Lake Dr N, Seattle, WA 98115
attractions.hours: 4:00 AM - 11:30 PM daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 2.8-mile paved car-free inner loop path for walking, running, and cycling
Two lifeguarded swimming beaches (West and East beaches)
Multiple sports facilities including tennis, basketball, baseball/softball, and soccer fields
Green Lake Small Craft Center for boating with hand carry boat launches and fishing piers
Community center, aquatic center, and Evans Pool
Wading pools, spray park, and playgrounds for children
attractions.tips: The inner loop path has designated lanes: pedestrians use the inside lane (closer to the lake), wheeled users use the outside lane at a 10 mph speed limit. Keep dogs leashed (max 5 feet). Water quality testing resumes in mid-May 2026 — check King County Public Health for swim beach conditions. Visit during early morning or weekday evenings to avoid crowds on the popular path.
attractions.nearby: Woodland Park Zoo, Green Lake Community Center and Evans Pool, Seattle Public Theatre (Bathhouse Theatre), Woodland Park, Aurora Avenue shopping district, Fremont neighborhood, Gas Works Park

attractions.name: Henry Art Gallery
attractions.description: The Henry Art Gallery is a museum for contemporary art and ideas located on the University of Washington campus. Founded in 1927, it was the first public art museum in Washington State. The museum features rotating exhibitions of contemporary art, diverse public programs, and a café, all grounded in the belief that art has the power to transform lives and communities.
attractions.address: 15th Avenue NE + NE 41st Street, Seattle, WA 98195
attractions.hours: Monday – Wednesday: Closed; Thursday: 10 AM – 7 PM; Friday – Sunday: 10 AM – 5 PM. Last entry 30 minutes before closing. Closed on Juneteenth, Independence Day, Labor Day, Veteran's Day, Thanksgiving Day, Christmas Day, and New Year's Day. Closes at 3 PM on Christmas Eve and New Year's Eve.
attractions.admission: Free for all visitors. Free public access supported in part by 4Culture.
highlights: First public art museum in Washington State, founded in 1927
Contemporary art exhibitions featuring emerging and established artists
Located on the University of Washington campus
City Grind Café on-site
Free admission for all visitors
Diverse public programs including lectures, workshops, and film screenings
attractions.tips: Visit on a Thursday for extended evening hours until 7 PM. Parking is free on Saturday afternoons and Sundays at the UW Central Parking Garage. Take the Link Light Rail to the U District Station (8 min walk) or University of Washington Station (15 min walk). Check the website before visiting as the museum is closed on several holidays. Photography is allowed in galleries without flash.
attractions.nearby: University of Washington campus, Burke-Gilman Trail, University Village shopping center, Frosh Pond, UW Botanic Gardens, Husky Stadium

attractions.name: Hiram M. Chittenden Locks (Ballard Locks)
attractions.description: The Hiram M. Chittenden Locks, also known as the Ballard Locks, is a complex of locks at the west end of Salmon Bay in Seattle, connecting the waters of Lake Washington, Lake Union, and Salmon Bay to the tidal waters of Puget Sound. Built between 1911–1917 by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, it carries more boat traffic than any other lock in the U.S. The site features a fish ladder, Carl S. English Jr. Botanical Gardens, and a visitor center, attracting over one million visitors annually. The locks are listed on the National Register of Historic Places and designated as a National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark.
attractions.hours: Grounds: 7:00 a.m. - 9:00 p.m. daily. Fish Ladder Viewing Room: 7:00 a.m. - 8:45 p.m. daily. Visitor Center: Wed-Sun 10:00 a.m. - 3:00 p.m. (Oct-Apr), Wed-Sun 10:00 a.m. - 6:00 p.m. (May-Sept). Free public tours at 2:00 p.m. May-Sept on open days.
attractions.days_open: Grounds and Fish Ladder: Daily. Visitor Center: Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday (closed Monday and Tuesday).
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Watch boats transit through the large and small locks
View salmon migration through the fish ladder windows (best June-September)
Carl S. English Jr. Botanical Gardens with over 300 plant species
Visitor Center with interpretive exhibits and gift shop
Free ranger-led public tours (May-September at 2:00 p.m.)
Historic National Register landmark built 1911-1917
attractions.tips: Best time to see salmon at the fish ladder is June through September. Parking costs $2/hour (max 3 hours) Monday-Saturday 8 a.m.-6 p.m. Arrive early for free public tours which start at 2:00 p.m. and last about one hour. Visit on a weekday to avoid crowds. Bring binoculars for better fish ladder viewing. The visitor center is closed Monday and Tuesday.
attractions.nearby: Carl S. English Jr. Botanical Gardens, Golden Gardens Park, Ballard Farmers Market, National Nordic Museum, Ballard Avenue Historic District, Shilshole Bay Marina

attractions.name: Hiram M. Chittenden Locks (Ballard Locks) Salmon Ladder
attractions.description: The Hiram M. Chittenden Locks, locally known as the Ballard Locks, is a complex of locks at the west end of Salmon Bay in Seattle, connecting the freshwater lakes of Washington and Union to the saltwater of Puget Sound. Completed in 1917 by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the locks are the busiest in the nation with over 45,000 vessels passing through annually. An adjacent fish ladder allows migrating salmon to travel upstream to spawn, making it one of Seattle's most popular tourist attractions and a designated National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark.
attractions.hours: Grounds: 7:00 AM - 9:00 PM daily. Fish Ladder Viewing Room: 7:00 AM - 8:45 PM daily. Visitor Center: Oct 1 - Apr 30: Wed-Sun 10:00 AM - 3:00 PM (closed Mon-Tue); May 1 - Sep 30: Wed-Sun 10:00 AM - 6:00 PM (closed Mon-Tue). Free daily tours at 2:00 PM (May 1 - Sep 30).
attractions.days_open: Grounds and Fish Ladder: Daily. Visitor Center: Wednesday through Sunday (closed Monday and Tuesday).
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Fish Ladder Viewing Room with underwater windows to watch migrating salmon (Chinook, Coho, Sockeye)
Two locks (small and large) handling the busiest lock traffic in the U.S.
Carl S. English Jr. Botanical Gardens with 7 acres of plant collections
Visitor Center with educational exhibits and historical displays
Free daily 2:00 PM guided tours (May through September)
Designated National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark and National Register of Historic Places
attractions.tips: Best salmon viewing is June through September: Chinook peak in late August, Coho peak in late September, Sockeye peak in July. The fish ladder may close for maintenance for ~2 weeks in mid-May. Visit on sunny days for the best views. The Visitor Center is closed Mon-Tue but grounds are open daily. Free parking is limited; arrive early or expect to pay $2/hour at nearby city pay stations. Take the free 2:00 PM guided tour for the full experience.
attractions.nearby: Ballard neighborhood (NW Market Street shops and restaurants), Golden Gardens Park, Shilshole Bay Marina, Ballard Locks Salmon Education Center, Hiram M. Chittenden Locks Visitor Center, Lockspot Cafe, Magnolia neighborhood, Salmon Bay Park

attractions.name: Kerry Park
attractions.description: Kerry Park is a small public park and viewpoint perched on the south slope of Queen Anne Hill in Seattle, Washington. It offers the most iconic panoramic views of the Seattle skyline, with the Space Needle prominently centered, Elliott Bay to the west, and Mount Rainier in the background on clear days. The park was donated to the city in 1927 by lumberman Albert S. Kerry and his wife Katharine so that all who stop there may enjoy the view.
attractions.hours: Monday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Tuesday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Wednesday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Thursday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Friday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Saturday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Sunday: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Iconic panoramic view of the Seattle skyline with the Space Needle at center
Unobstructed views of Elliott Bay, Mount Rainier, and downtown Seattle
Changing Form sculpture by artist Doris Totten Chase, installed in 1971
Coin-operated telescopes for closer viewing of landmarks
Benches positioned to face the south-facing skyline view
Small children's playground accessible via stairway below the park
attractions.tips: Visit at sunset or after dark for the best photos when the city lights up. Arrive early to find street parking as the surrounding residential area has limited spaces. Keep noise levels down out of respect for neighbors. The view is clearest on winter days when trees have lost their leaves. No bathrooms are available at the park. Bring quarters for the coin-operated telescopes.
attractions.nearby: Space Needle (0.8 mi), Chihuly Garden and Glass (0.8 mi), Museum of Pop Culture (0.8 mi), Pacific Science Center (0.8 mi), Seattle Center (0.8 mi), Pike Place Market (1.7 mi), Amazon Spheres (1.3 mi), Museum of History & Industry (1.1 mi)

attractions.name: Klondike Gold Rush - Seattle Unit, National Historical Park
attractions.description: Klondike Gold Rush - Seattle Unit is a National Historical Park located in Seattle's Pioneer Square neighborhood. It commemorates Seattle's role as the primary supply center during the 1897-1898 Klondike Gold Rush, when merchants supplied prospectors heading to the Yukon Territory. The visitor center features exhibits, a museum-quality film, ranger programs, and rotating art and history exhibits in the Kerr Room.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Historic gold rush exhibits and artifacts in the Pioneer Square neighborhood
Educational museum-quality film about the Klondike Gold Rush
Rotating exhibits in the Kerr Room featuring art, history, and culture
Free Junior Ranger program for children
Historic District Walking Tours of Pioneer Square
Trails and Rails programs on the Coast Starlight Amtrak train
attractions.tips: The park is closed Monday and Tuesday, so plan your visit for Wednesday through Sunday. It is located in Seattle's Pioneer Square neighborhood near the waterfront and is easily accessible by public transit. Allow 1-2 hours to explore the exhibits and watch the park film. The visitor center is fully accessible. Check the calendar for special ranger programs and temporary exhibits.
attractions.nearby: Pioneer Square Historic District, Seattle Art Museum, Waterfront Park, Great Wheel, Smith Tower, Seattle Aquarium, Columbia Center Sky View Observatory, Underground Tour

attractions.name: Lake Union Park
attractions.description: Lake Union Park is a 12-acre (4.9 ha) urban park located at the south end of Lake Union in Seattle's South Lake Union neighborhood. Owned by the City of Seattle and operated by Seattle Parks and Recreation, the park provides access to green space and water while celebrating the city's cultural, maritime, and industrial heritage. It is home to the Museum of History and Industry (MOHAI) and the Center for Wooden Boats, along with several historic vessels at its wharf.
attractions.hours: Open daily during daylight hours (approximately 4:00 AM to 11:30 PM year-round; specific facilities like MOHAI and the Center for Wooden Boats have their own hours). Restrooms and drinking water available during operating hours at the MOHAI Cafe, near the Center for Wooden Boats, and at the public boat launch.
attractions.admission: Free (park access is free; MOHAI and the Center for Wooden Boats have separate admission fees)
highlights: Museum of History and Industry (MOHAI) - largest private heritage organization in Washington state
Center for Wooden Boats - hands-on maritime museum with rowboat rentals
Historic Ships Wharf featuring the Steamship Virginia V, Tugboat Arthur Foss, Lightship Swiftsure, and Fireboat Duwamish
Model Boat Pond for watching model sailing vessels
Kayak Float for paddling access to the park
Footbridge connecting the park to South Lake Union neighborhood with scenic views
attractions.tips: Parking is limited with one pay lot on-site; consider taking the Seattle Streetcar or using public transit. Restrooms are available at MOHAI Cafe, near the Center for Wooden Boats, and at the public boat launch. The closest ATM is at Art Marble 21 across the footbridge on Westlake Avenue. A children's playground is located just east of the park. The park hosts outdoor festivals and events throughout the year. Some areas may be closed for maintenance improvements through early June 2026.

attractions.name: Lincoln Park
attractions.description: Lincoln Park is Seattle's largest city park, spanning 135 acres along the Puget Sound shoreline in West Seattle. It features 4.6 miles of walking paths, 3.9 miles of bike trails, a wading pool (Colman Pool), play areas, sports fields, picnic sites, and hand carry boat launches. The park offers stunning views of the Olympic Mountains and Puget Sound, with art installations and diverse natural habitats including old-growth forest.
attractions.admission: Free

4.6 miles of walking paths and 3.9 miles of bike trails
Colman Pool wading pool and spray park
Sports fields including soccer, football, and baseball/softball
Hand carry boat launch at 300 feet of shoreline
Art in the Park installations and playground areas
attractions.tips: Visit early morning or late afternoon for the best views of the Olympic Mountains and Puget Sound. Colman Pool (wading pool) is open seasonally in summer. The park can get crowded on weekends, so arrive early for picnic sites. Bring layers as the waterfront location can be windy. Parking is available but fills up quickly on nice days.

attractions.name: Museum of History & Industry (MOHAI)
attractions.description: The Museum of History & Industry (MOHAI) is the largest private heritage organization in Washington state, dedicated to preserving and sharing the history of Seattle and the greater Puget Sound region. Located in the historic Naval Reserve Armory at Lake Union Park, MOHAI houses a collection of nearly four million artifacts, photographs, and archival materials. Key exhibits include Boeing's first commercial plane, the 1919 Boeing B-1, the Petticoat Flag, and the Rainier Brewing Company's 12-foot neon "R" sign.
attractions.hours: Daily: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM. First Thursdays of the month: free admission to core exhibits from 5:00 PM – 8:00 PM. Reduced hours: April 22 closes at 3:00 PM.

Rainier Brewing Company's 12-foot neon 'R' sign

Petticoat Flag sewn by women during the 1856 Battle of Seattle
Historic Naval Reserve Armory building at Lake Union Park
attractions.tips: Visit on the first Thursday of the month for free admission to core exhibits from 5-8 PM. Youth 14 and under enter free. Consider becoming a member if you plan multiple visits. Check the website for special exhibit ticket releases, as some exhibits like the Freedom Plane tour require advance reservation. The museum offers multilingual welcome sheets in nine languages. Parking is available on-site and nearby. The Gourmondo Café and MOHAI Mercantile gift shop are on-site amenities.

attractions.name: Museum of Pop Culture (MoPOP)
attractions.description: The Museum of Pop Culture (MoPOP) is a nonprofit museum dedicated to contemporary popular culture, housed in a striking Frank Gehry-designed building at Seattle Center. With over 80,000 artifacts and 140,000 square feet of exhibition space across three floors, MoPOP celebrates music, science fiction, fantasy, horror, and gaming through immersive, interactive exhibits. Highlights include the Guitar Gallery, Sound Lab, and rotating exhibitions on topics from Jimi Hendrix to hip-hop culture.

attractions.name: Myrtle Edwards Park
attractions.description: Myrtle Edwards Park features a 1.25-mile winding bike and pedestrian path along Elliott Bay with fantastic views of the Olympic Mountains, Mount Rainier, and Puget Sound. Located just north of downtown Seattle, it offers easy access from the city center and connects to bike paths leading to Magnolia. The park is known for its waterfront promenade, public art installations, and scenic shoreline views.

Bring layers as it can be windy along the waterfront.

attractions.name: National Nordic Museum
attractions.description: The National Nordic Museum is a museum in the Ballard neighborhood of Seattle, Washington, dedicated to Nordic history, art, culture, and the heritage of Nordic immigrants to the United States. Founded in 1980 and designated as the National Nordic Museum by Congress in 2019, the institution features a core exhibit exploring 12,000 years of Nordic life with objects from all five Nordic countries. The museum also serves as a community gathering place, offering exhibitions, educational programs, concerts, lectures, and cultural events.

attractions.name: Olympic Sculpture Park
attractions.description: The Olympic Sculpture Park is a free-admission outdoor sculpture park operated by the Seattle Art Museum, featuring monumental modern and contemporary sculptures on Seattle's waterfront. Opened in 2007 on a former industrial brownfield site, the 9-acre park overlooks Elliott Bay and the Olympic Mountains with a Z-shaped design connecting four distinct Pacific Northwest landscapes. The park includes works by renowned artists such as Richard Serra, Alexander Calder, Claes Oldenburg, Teresita Fernández, and Tony Smith, along with the Neukom Vivarium and a restored salmon habitat beach.
attractions.hours: Olympic Sculpture Park: Opens 30 minutes before sunrise, closes 30 minutes after sunset (free and open 365 days a year). Neukom Vivarium: May–October, Saturday & Sunday (hours subject to volunteer availability).
attractions.admission: Free

Seattle Cloud Cover — a laminated glass bridge by Teresita Fernández displaying images of the sky
Neukom Vivarium — a greenhouse-like structure by Mark Dion enclosing a 60-foot Western hemlock nurse log

Free smartphone audio tours are available at visitsam.org/explore. Public guided tours run most weekends at 1–2 pm. Don't touch or climb on sculptures. Bicycles and scooters must be walked through the park. Pets are welcome on a 6-foot leash. Pay parking is available in the PACCAR Pavilion garage (open 6 am–11 pm daily). The park is easily accessible by foot, bike, or transit from downtown Seattle.

attractions.name: Original Starbucks
attractions.description: The Original Starbucks, also known as the Pike Place Starbucks store, is the first Starbucks location, established on March 30, 1971 at Pike Place Market in downtown Seattle. Founded by Gordon Bowker, Jerry Baldwin, and Zev Siegl, it originally sold whole bean coffee, tea, and spices. The store has maintained its early appearance over time and is a major tourist attraction, drawing crowds of visitors who want to experience where the global coffee chain began.

attractions.name: Pacific Science Center
attractions.description: Pacific Science Center (PacSci) is a nonprofit science education organization in Seattle, Washington. Founded in 1962, it reused the United States Science Pavilion built for the Century 21 Exposition (World's Fair). The 7.1-acre campus on Seattle Center features hands-on exhibits, an IMAX theater, planetarium, laser dome, and tropical butterfly house, attracting over 1 million visitors annually.

attractions.name: Pike Place Market
attractions.description: Pike Place Market is a public market in Seattle, Washington, established in 1907. It is one of the oldest continuously operated public farmers' markets in the United States, overlooking the Elliott Bay waterfront on Puget Sound. The market serves as a place of business for many small farmers, craftspeople, and merchants, and is Seattle's most popular tourist destination with more than 20 million annual visitors. It spans a nine-acre historic district with 10 levels of shops, restaurants, and attractions.

attractions.name: Pioneer Square
attractions.description: Pioneer Square is Seattle's original neighborhood and the city's oldest historic district, dating back to 1852. The area features Richardsonian Romanesque architecture from the rebuilding after the Great Seattle Fire of 1889, including the iconic Pioneer Building, totem pole, and iron pergola. Today it is a vibrant destination known for its art galleries, underground tours, restaurants, nightlife, and the Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week (public outdoor area and parks). Individual businesses, museums, and tours have their own hours.

The area can have a visible homeless population; keep belongings secure and stay aware of surroundings, especially after dark.

The museum operates three facilities: its main museum in downtown Seattle, the Seattle Asian Art Museum in Volunteer Park on Capitol Hill, and Olympic Sculpture Park on the central Seattle waterfront. SAM houses a collection of more than 25,000 works of art from around the world, spanning cultures and centuries. The museum features rotating exhibitions, extensive permanent collections, and diverse programming including tours, events, and educational programs.

attractions.name: Seattle Center

It serves as a gathering place for community events, festivals, arts, and culture, featuring the Space Needle, Climate Pledge Arena, Museum of Pop Culture, and numerous other attractions. The center hosts hundreds of events annually and is a defining destination for both locals and visitors.

The wheel extends nearly 40 feet out over Elliott Bay and is covered in over 500,000 LED lights for spectacular nighttime light shows.

attractions.name: Seattle Waterfront
attractions.description: The Seattle Waterfront is a vibrant waterfront district along Elliott Bay in downtown Seattle, Washington. It stretches from Pioneer Square north through Downtown to Belltown, featuring historic piers, shops, restaurants, attractions like the Seattle Great Wheel and Seattle Aquarium, cruise ship terminals, ferry docks, and the iconic Ferris wheel. Once the hub of Seattle's maritime activity, the area has been transformed into a premier recreational and retail destination with parks, dining, and entertainment while retaining its nautical heritage.
attractions.address: Alaskan Way (along Elliott Bay from Pioneer Square to Belltown)
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (waterfront district is publicly accessible at all times)
attractions.admission: Free (individual attractions along the waterfront may charge admission)
attractions.phone: N/A (waterfront district; individual businesses have their own numbers)
highlights: Seattle Great Wheel - iconic Ferris wheel with panoramic views
Seattle Aquarium - marine life exhibits and interactive displays
Historic piers (54-70) with shops, restaurants, and events
Olympic Sculpture Park - free outdoor art museum at the northern end
Washington State Ferry Terminal and cruise ship docks
Alaskan Way promenade with walking paths and scenic views
attractions.tips: Visit during summer months for the best weather and most activities. The waterfront is free to walk and explore — budget for parking or use public transit. Many attractions like the Aquarium and Great Wheel have separate admission fees. Check the waterfront events calendar for festivals, farmers markets, and special events. Arrive early during cruise ship season to avoid crowds. The area is wheelchair accessible with paved promenades.
attractions.nearby: Pike Place Market, Seattle Art Museum, Pioneer Square, Space Needle, Underground Tour, Westlake Center, Seattle Central Library

attractions.name: Seward Park
attractions.description: Seward Park is a 300-acre municipal park in Seattle, Washington, located on a peninsula jutting into Lake Washington. Named after U.S. Secretary of State William H. Seward, it features one of the last remaining tracts of old-growth forest within Seattle city limits. The park offers a 2.4-mile loop trail, beaches, an environmental center, an art studio, and an amphitheater. It was designed by the Olmsted Brothers and is a beloved destination for hiking, swimming, and wildlife viewing.
attractions.hours: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 300 acres of old-growth forest, one of the last within Seattle city limits
2.4-mile bike and walking loop trail around the peninsula
Beaches and shoreline on Lake Washington for swimming
Seward Park Environmental & Audubon Center
Amphitheater and art studio
Native plant garden and miles of hiking trails
attractions.tips: Visit in summer when Lake Washington Blvd S closes to cars for a car-free walking and biking experience. Bring swimwear for the beaches. The loop trail is paved and accessible. Arrive early on weekends as parking fills up quickly. Check the Audubon Center for nature programs and events.
attractions.nearby: Columbia City, Mount Baker neighborhood, I-90 Trail, Washington Park Arboretum, Genesee Park, Lake Washington, Lake Washington Boulevard scenic drive

attractions.name: Space Needle
attractions.description: The Space Needle is an iconic observation tower in Seattle, Washington, built for the 1962 World's Fair. Standing 605 feet tall, it offers 360-degree panoramic views of downtown Seattle, Mount Rainier, the Olympic and Cascade Mountains, and Puget Sound. The tower features the Loupe, a rotating glass floor observation level, and the SkyCity restaurant.

attractions.admission: General Admission: $37.50 (Morning, before 10 AM) | $49.00 (Day, 10 AM – 10 PM) | $42.50 (Night, after 10 PM). Combo tickets and Loupe Lounge access: $69–$139. Discounts available for youth (ages 5–12) and seniors (65+). Prices subject to change; see [Space Needle Buy Tickets](https://www.spaceneedle.com/buy-tickets) for current rates.
highlights: 360-degree panoramic observation deck at 520 feet
The Loupe — world's first and only rotating glass floor
SkyCity restaurant with sweeping city views
Legacy Light (Skybeam) — 85 million candela beam visible for miles
Fast elevators reaching the top in 41 seconds
Views of Mount Rainier, Olympic and Cascade Mountains, and Puget Sound
attractions.tips: Visit at sunset for the best views of both daylight and city lights. Book timed-entry tickets online in advance, especially for weekends. Allow 30 minutes to get from entrance to the top including security screening. Consider the combo ticket with Chihuly Garden and Glass for a full day at Seattle Center. Take the Seattle Center Monorail from downtown for a fun, convenient ride. Small bags and purses are allowed; large bags require free bag check.
attractions.nearby: Chihuly Garden and Glass, Pacific Science Center, Museum of Pop Culture (MoPOP), Climate Pledge Arena, Seattle Center Armory, Seattle Children's Theatre, Fisher Pavilion

attractions.name: The Center for Wooden Boats
attractions.description: The Center for Wooden Boats is a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit maritime museum located in South Lake Union's Lake Union Park in Seattle. Its mission is to create a thriving community that fosters equitable access to empowering maritime experiences through wooden boat preservation, education, and hands-on sailing programs. Visitors can explore exhibits, take free rowboat rentals, participate in sailing lessons, rent wooden sailboats and kayaks, and book charter cruises on historic vessels.
attractions.hours: Winter (Jan 3–Mar 13): Wed–Sun 10:00 AM–4:00 PM | Spring (Mar 14–Apr 15): Wed–Sun 11:30 AM–7:00 PM | Summer (Apr 16–Sep 9): Wed–Sun 12:30 PM–8:00 PM | Autumn (Sep 10–Oct 31): Wed–Sun 11:30 AM–7:00 PM | Winter (Nov 1–Mar 19, 2027): Wed–Sun 10:00 AM–4:00 PM. Last boat out 90 minutes before closing. Closed July 4, September 11–13, November 26, and December 14–January 2.
attractions.admission: Free. Admission to The Center for Wooden Boats is always free. Boat rentals, sailing lessons, and charter cruises have separate fees.
highlights: Free one-hour peapod rowboat rentals for all visitors
Fleet of classic wooden sailboats, canoes, kayaks, SUPs, and row boats for rent
Signature sailing programs and sailing lessons for adults and families
90-minute charter cruises on historic vessels
Woodworking and boatbuilding workshops and classes
Located inside the scenic Lake Union Park
attractions.tips: Admission is always free, making it an excellent budget-friendly family outing. Arrive early on summer weekends as free rowboat rentals are popular and may have wait times. Boat rentals and sailing programs require reservations — book ahead. Check seasonal hours as they vary throughout the year. The last boat out is 90 minutes before closing. Free programs include peapod rowboat rentals and Sunday Public Sail events. Parking is available in Lake Union Park's paid lot.

attractions.name: The Museum of Flight
attractions.description: The Museum of Flight is a private non-profit air and space museum located in Seattle, Washington. It is the largest private air and space museum in the world, featuring an extensive collection of aircraft and spacecraft. Established in 1965, the museum is fully accredited by the American Alliance of Museums and hosts large K-12 educational programs serving more than 140,000 students annually.
attractions.hours: Daily 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adult: $29.00 | Youth (5-17): $21.00 | Senior (65+): $25.00 | Child (4 and under): Free | Members: Free. Discounts available for AAA members, Boeing employees, active-duty military, and veterans.
highlights: Largest private air and space museum in the world
Air Force One presidential aircraft on display
Concorde supersonic passenger jet
Apollo command module and space artifacts
Restoration facility visible to visitors
Challenger Learning Center for STEM education
attractions.tips: Allow at least 3-4 hours to fully explore the museum. Free parking is available on-site. Book tickets online in advance, especially for weekends and holidays. Members get free admission and priority entry. The museum is wheelchair accessible. Consider visiting on a weekday for smaller crowds.
attractions.nearby: Tukwila International Boulevard, Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, Southcenter Mall, Boeing Renton Factory, Fort Dent Park, Duwamish River Trail

attractions.name: University of Washington Botanic Gardens (Washington Park Arboretum)
attractions.description: The University of Washington Botanic Gardens, centered on the Washington Park Arboretum, is a 230-acre living museum of plant collections managed by the University of Washington's College of the Environment. It features a world-class collection of woody plants, the Japanese Garden, and the Graham Visitors Center, offering trails, tours, and educational programs. The Arboretum is a beloved Seattle destination for walking, birdwatching, and experiencing nature across the seasons.
attractions.hours: Graham Visitors Center & Gift Shop: Tuesday–Sunday, 10:00 a.m.–4:00 p.m. (closed Mondays). GVC restrooms: Daily 9:00 a.m.–5:00 p.m. Arboretum grounds: Open daily, dawn to dusk.
attractions.admission: Free — The Arboretum grounds and Seattle Japanese Garden are free to the public. Tram tours and some special events may have fees.
highlights: 230 acres of curated plant collections including rhododendrons, conifers, and perennials
Seattle Japanese Garden — a 3.5-acre authentic Japanese landscape garden
Graham Visitors Center with maps, gift shop, and exhibits
Tram tours offering guided seasonal overviews of the Arboretum
2.5 miles of walking trails and the famed Azalea Way
Wisteria Hall event venue and Center for Urban Horticulture
attractions.tips: Visit in spring for peak azalea and rhododendron blooms (April–May), or in fall for stunning foliage colors. The grounds are free and open dawn to dusk — great for early morning walks or sunset strolls. Wear comfortable walking shoes and bring a map from the Graham Visitors Center. Parking can be limited on weekends; consider biking or taking public transit. Dogs are allowed on trails but must be leashed.
attractions.nearby: Seattle Japanese Garden (within the Arboretum), Washington Park, Madison Park Beach, Volunteer Park, Washington Park Golf Course, U-District, Burke-Gilman Trail

attractions.name: University of Washington Seattle
attractions.description: The University of Washington Seattle is a premier public research university founded in 1861, making it one of the oldest universities on the West Coast. The 703-acre main campus features iconic Collegiate Gothic architecture, cherry blossom-lined pathways, and stunning views of Mount Rainier and Lake Washington. It is home to world-class museums, libraries, and research facilities, and is consistently ranked among the top public universities in the world.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily; individual buildings and facilities have varying schedules
attractions.admission: Free (campus grounds open to the public; some museums and facilities may charge admission)
highlights: Iconic Collegiate Gothic architecture including the Suzzallo Library
Cherry blossom trees along the Quad and Rainier Vista
Suzzallo Library — one of the most beautiful university libraries in the world
Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture
Henry Art Gallery — UW's contemporary art museum
University of Washington Medical Center and research facilities
attractions.tips: Visit during spring (late March to early April) to see the famous cherry blossoms in full bloom along the Quad. The Suzzallo Library's Reading Room is open to visitors during posted hours. Take the free campus shuttle to get around the large campus. Don't miss the view from the Drumheller Fountain area on a clear day for Mount Rainier vistas. The University District (The Ave) just north of campus offers many dining and shopping options.

attractions.name: Volunteer Park
attractions.description: Volunteer Park is a 48.3-acre public park located in the Capitol Hill neighborhood of Seattle. Designated as a Seattle Landmark, it is home to the Volunteer Park Conservatory, the Seattle Asian Art Museum, and a historic water tower with panoramic city views. The park features gardens, trails, tennis courts, a wading pool, and art installations including Isamu Noguchi's Black Sun sculpture.
attractions.hours: 4:00 AM – 11:30 PM daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Volunteer Park Conservatory - historic glass greenhouse with tropical plants
Seattle Asian Art Museum - art deco building with Asian art collections
Historic water tower with 360-degree panoramic views of Seattle
Black Sun sculpture by renowned artist Isamu Noguchi
Dahlia Garden maintained by the Puget Sound Dahlia Association since 1984
Tennis courts, wading pool, and children's play area
attractions.tips: Climb the Volunteer Park Water Tower for free panoramic views of the Seattle skyline, Mount Rainier, and Puget Sound. Visit the Conservatory on weekends for the best experience. The Dahlia Garden is at its peak in late summer. Arrive early on sunny weekends as parking can be limited.

attractions.name: Washington Park Arboretum
attractions.description: The Washington Park Arboretum is a 230-acre public botanical garden and arboretum on the shores of Lake Washington in Seattle. Jointly managed by the University of Washington and the City of Seattle, it features world-class plant collections including oaks, maples, and camellias. The grounds include miles of walking trails, wetlands, woodlands, and the Seattle Japanese Garden.
attractions.hours: Every day: Dawn to 8:00 PM. Graham Visitors Center: Wednesday through Sunday, 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM.
attractions.admission: Free (grounds open to public free of charge). Seattle Japanese Garden within the Arboretum has a separate admission fee.

World-class plant collections including oaks, maples, camellias, and azaleas
Seattle Japanese Garden (3.5-acre traditional Japanese stroll garden)

Graham Visitors Center with tram tours, public restrooms, and event space
Year-round events including luminaria walks and public tours
attractions.tips: Visit at dawn or dusk for the best lighting and fewer crowds. Wear comfortable walking shoes as trails can be muddy, especially near Marsh Island. Dogs are welcome on leash. The Graham Visitors Center has restrooms and a drinking fountain. Free guided tram tours are offered on the first Thursday of each month. Parking is available along Arboretum Drive but can be limited on weekends.

attractions.name: Wing Luke Museum of the Asian Pacific American Experience
attractions.description: The Wing Luke Museum is the only pan-Asian Pacific American community-based museum in the United States, focusing on the culture, art, and history of Asian Pacific Americans. Established in 1967 and located in Seattle's Chinatown-International District, it houses over 18,000 items in its collections and is a Smithsonian Institution affiliate. The museum is housed in the historic East Kong Yick Building and is affiliated with the U.S. National Park Service.
attractions.hours: Wednesday - Sunday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM. Closed Monday and Tuesday.
attractions.admission: Museum Members: Free; Adults: $24.95; Seniors (62+): $21.95; Students (13-18 or with ID): $17.95; Children (5-12): $14.95; Children under 5: Free. Join a complimentary Historic Hotel Tour (Wed-Sun at 10:30 AM, 11:45 AM, 1:30 PM, and 3:30 PM) on a first-come, first-served basis. EBT cardholders get free admission for up to five guests. The museum is easily accessible via the Link Light Rail Chinatown-International District station. Street parking is free on Sundays and holidays.

attractions.name: Woodland Park Zoo
attractions.description: Woodland Park Zoo is a 92-acre zoological park in Seattle's Phinney Ridge neighborhood, home to over 1,100 animals representing 300 species. Founded in 1899, it is one of the oldest zoos in the western United States and is accredited by the Association of Zoos and Aquariums (AZA). The zoo is renowned for its naturalistic exhibits, conservation programs, and commitment to saving wildlife.

Interactive experiences including animal encounters and behind-the-scenes tours

attractions.name: Bellevue Arts Museum
attractions.description: The Bellevue Arts Museum (BAM) is a museum dedicated to contemporary visual art, craft, and design in Bellevue, Washington. Founded in 1975 from an arts and crafts fair tradition dating back to 1947, it occupied a distinctive three-story building designed by architect Steven Holl starting in 2001. The museum closed indefinitely to the public on September 4, 2024, due to financial difficulties and is currently under receivership, with plans to sell the building in 2026 while continuing signature events like the annual BAM Arts Fair.

Established in 1992 from an initial 7-acre gift, it showcases Pacific Northwest plants, demonstration gardens, and horticultural techniques. It serves as an urban refuge offering beauty, serenity, learning, and seasonal events like plant sales, concerts, and holiday lights.

Suspension bridge and Ravine Experience trails
Demonstration of Pacific Northwest native plants and sustainable gardening

No pets (except service animals), bikes, or skateboards allowed; stay on paths. Stroller-friendly in most areas. Visit Trillium Store and Copper Kettle Coffee (seasonal). Free public tours on weekends April-October.

Educational programs, tours, camps, and annual events like farm fair

Story Tree reading nooks and stage

Home to events like summer concerts, July 4th celebrations, and Kirkland Uncorked, it offers stunning views of the lake and Seattle skyline.

attractions.name: Marymoor Park

It offers diverse recreational activities including sports fields, a velodrome, rock climbing wall, 40-acre off-leash dog park, radio control aircraft field, walking and biking trails connected to the Sammamish River Trail, community gardens, and historic sites like Clise Mansion. The park hosts summer concerts, outdoor movies, and various events year-round.King County Parks, Wikipedia

attractions.name: Mercer Slough Nature Park

attractions.name: Boeing Future of Flight
attractions.description: Boeing Future of Flight is an aviation museum and education center located at Paine Field in Mukilteo, Washington, showcasing Boeing's story through interactive exhibits on current and emerging aerospace technologies including space, hypersonics, sustainable aviation, and autonomous systems. It serves as the gateway to the Boeing Everett Factory Tour, offering an 80-minute guided experience inside the world's largest manufacturing facility to view 777/777X assembly. Visitors can enjoy planespotting from the Sky Deck overlooking active Paine Field airport and explore the flagship Boeing Store.Boeing Future of Flight Visit, Wikipedia
attractions.hours: Monday–Sunday: 8:30am–5:30pm (PST). Closed holidays: 11/26, 12/24, 12/25, 12/31, 1/1.
attractions.admission: General Admission: Adult $14, Youth (6-15) $7, Child (5 & under) Free, Senior/Military $12, Boeing Employee $6. Factory Tour (includes GA): Adult $42-46, Youth $30-32, Child (5 & under) Free, Senior/Military $36-37, Boeing Employee $20. Children/Youth must be 4ft tall for tour; advance purchase recommended.[Boeing Future of Flight Visit](https://www.boeingfutureofflight.com/visit)
highlights: Gateway to Boeing Everett Factory Tour (world's largest building by volume)
Interactive exhibits on future aviation, space, hypersonics, sustainable fuels
Sky Deck for planespotting at Paine Field
Hands-on Kids' Zone and Education Lab with robots
Flagship Boeing Store with aerospace merchandise
Wheelchair accessible with special accommodations available
attractions.tips: Book tickets in advance online or by phone as tours sell out quickly. Arrive 30+ minutes early for tours. No bags/phones/cameras/food on tour (free lockers provided). Children under 16 need adult; 4ft height min for tour. Use restrooms before tour. Free parking on-site.[Boeing Future of Flight Tour](https://www.boeingfutureofflight.com/tour), [Visit Seattle](https://visitseattle.org/members/boeing-future-of-flight-pd/)
attractions.nearby: Boeing Everett Factory (adjacent, toured from here), Paine Field Airport (plane spotting), Museum of Flight (Seattle, ~25 miles south).[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_Flight_Aviation_Center_&_Boeing_Tour)

attractions.name: Chateau Ste. Michelle
attractions.description: Chateau Ste. Michelle is Washington's oldest winery, founded in 1934, located on a historic 105-acre estate in Woodinville resembling a French chateau. It produces award-winning wines including Chardonnay, Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Riesling, and offers curated tasting experiences, tours, and summer concerts. The grounds feature beautiful gardens, peacocks, and ponds for visitors to explore.https://www.ste-michelle.com, Wikipedia
attractions.hours: Wed-Sun 11am-5pm; Fri-Sat until 7pm (Twilight Hours; subject to early closures on event days)
attractions.admission: Grounds entry Free; Tastings from $50/person (Club $40); High Tea $75 (Club $60); Somm’s Table $110 (Club $88)
highlights: Washington's oldest winery (est. 1934)
Historic French-style chateau on National Register
Award-winning wines: Chardonnay, Riesling, Cabernet
Summer concert series at amphitheatre
Curated tastings and food pairings
Beautiful 105-acre grounds with gardens and peacocks
attractions.tips: Reserve tastings in advance especially weekends; stay hydrated between tastings; no outside food/beverages; designated driver or tour for safety; arrive early for walk-ins; check events calendar for closures.[https://www.ste-michelle.com/visit-us/contact-us](https://www.ste-michelle.com/visit-us/contact-us), [Woodinville Wine Country](https://woodinvillewinecountry.com/news/the-perfect-woodinville-wine-country-itinerary-for-first-time-visitors/)
attractions.nearby: Other Woodinville wineries (DeLille Cellars, Novelty Hill-Januik, Columbia Winery), Woodinville Whiskey Co., The Herbfarm restaurant, Redhook Brewery.[https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g58835-d126739-Chateau_Ste_Michelle_Winery-Woodinville_Washington.html](https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g58835-d126739-Chateau_Ste_Michelle_Winery-Woodinville_Washington.html)

attractions.name: Evergreen Arboretum and Gardens
attractions.description: Evergreen Arboretum & Gardens is a 3.5-acre public garden located in American Legion Memorial Park in Everett, Washington. It features twelve themed gardens such as the Japanese Maple Grove, Conifer Garden, Woodland Garden & Fernery, and Snohomish County Master Gardeners’ Demonstration Border. The site includes 23 sculptures by Northwest artists, ADA-approved pathways, and offers free docent-led tours, educational classes, and family events year-round. Maintained by volunteers and city parks, it provides seasonal beauty from dawn to dusk.Evergreen Arboretum Homepage
attractions.hours: Dawn until dusk
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 12 themed gardens including Japanese Maple Grove and Conifer Garden
23 sculptures by Northwest artists
ADA-approved pathways
Free educational classes and events
Volunteer-maintained with city support
Views of Port Gardner Bay
attractions.tips: Visit during spring for blooms or fall for maple colors; wear comfortable shoes for walking paths; check website for events and classes; free parking available; leashed dogs welcome; stay on paths to protect plants.[Evergreen Arboretum Hours](https://www.evergreenarboretum.com/hours)
attractions.nearby: American Legion Memorial Park (playground, sports fields, bay views), Legion Park Golf Course, Howarth Park (beach, trails), Mukilteo Lighthouse Park.[EverettWA Facilities](https://www.everettwa.gov/facilities/facility/details/Evergreen-Arboretum-Gardens-106)

attractions.name: Hibulb Cultural Center and Natural History Preserve
attractions.description: The Hibulb Cultural Center and Natural History Preserve is an interactive cultural center dedicated to reviving, restoring, and sharing the history, traditional values, and spiritual beliefs of the Tulalip Tribes, successors to the Snohomish, Snoqualmie, Skykomish, and allied bands of the 1855 Treaty of Point Elliott. It features a 23,000-square-foot facility with permanent and temporary exhibits, Canoe Hall with historic canoes and archaeology, a replica longhouse, research library, gift shop, and a 50-acre natural history preserve with forests, wetlands, and streams. Visitors can enjoy self-guided or group tours, educational programs, events, and demonstrations of Coast Salish culture.Hibulb Cultural Center, Hibulb Brochure
attractions.hours: Mon: Closed; Tue-Fri: 10 AM - 5 PM; Sat-Sun: 12 PM - 5 PM (First Thu monthly: free admission and extended hours)
attractions.admission: Adults (18-49): $10; Elders (50+): $7; Students (6-17)/Military/Veterans: $6; Children 5 & under: Free; Family (2 adults + up to 4 children): $25; Tribal/HCC Members: Free; Groups: special rates (call for info); Free first Thursday monthly (excl. holidays)
highlights: 23,000 sq ft interactive exhibits on Tulalip Tribes history and culture
Canoe Hall with historic canoes and Snohomish County archaeology
Replica longhouse with storytelling audio
50-acre natural history preserve with wetlands and salmon streams
State-certified archaeological repository (first tribal facility)
Research library, gift shop with Coast Salish art, and cultural events
attractions.tips: Allow 2-3 hours for exhibits and trails; visit first Thursday for free entry; check event schedule for workshops/demonstrations; wear comfortable shoes for preserve trails; tribal members free; photography restrictions may apply indoors.
attractions.nearby: Tulalip Resort Casino, Seattle Premium Outlets, Quil Ceda Creek Casino, Cabela's, Ebey Waterfront Park (Marysville), Jennings Memorial Nature Park.[Marysville Attractions](https://www.marysvillewa.gov/224/Attractions), [Tripadvisor](https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g7106558-d1102457-Tulalip_Resort_Casino-Tulalip_Washington.html)

attractions.name: Historic Downtown Snohomish
attractions.description: Historic Downtown Snohomish is a National Register Historic District listed in 1974, featuring well-preserved 19th and early 20th-century commercial and residential architecture along the Snohomish River. Known as the "Antique Capital of the Northwest," it offers a pedestrian-friendly area with antique shops, boutiques, restaurants, and riverfront trails. The district preserves the town's logging-era heritage while supporting vibrant small businesses and community events.Snohomish WA Official Website, Historic Downtown Snohomish Association, HistoryLink.org
attractions.hours: Varies by business; many shops open daily 10AM-5PM or 11AM-6PM, with extended hours on weekends
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Listed on National Register of Historic Places since 1974
Over 50 antique shops and boutiques
Snohomish Riverfront Trail for walking
Preserved Victorian and Craftsman architecture
Pedestrian-friendly with mature trees and alleys
Home to events like guided walking tours
attractions.tips: Visit on weekends for fullest shop openings and free parking at City Hall; wear comfortable shoes for walking the district and river trail; check individual shop hours as they vary; combine with lunch at local cafes.
attractions.nearby: Blackman House Museum (0.1 mi), Snohomish River Trail, Star Center Antique Mall, Skip Rock Distillery, Stocker Farms (nearby agricultural attraction), Bob Heirman Wildlife Preserve.[Tripadvisor](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g58747-d8420279-Reviews-Historic_Downtown_Snohomish-Snohomish_Washington.html), [HistoryLink Tours](https://historylink.tours/stop/blackman-house-museum/)

attractions.name: Imagine Children's Museum
attractions.description: Imagine Children's Museum is a hands-on, interactive children's museum in downtown Everett, WA, designed for children ages 1-12 and their families. It promotes learning through play with exhibits encouraging role-playing, experimentation, splashing, thinking, wandering, and creating in a safe environment. As the largest children's museum in the Pacific Northwest, it features three floors of play spaces following a recent expansion. Official site, FAQ
attractions.hours: Mon: CLOSED; Tue-Sat: 9AM-4PM; Sun: 9AM-4PM (members only 9-10AM) [Hours page](https://www.imaginecm.org/hours-admission/)
attractions.admission: General $22 (children/adults); Under 2 FREE; Military/Seniors $19; EBT $5 (4 entries). Half-price Tuesdays $11; Free 3:30-4PM. [Hours page](https://www.imaginecm.org/hours-admission/)
highlights: Largest children's museum in PNW with 3 floors of exhibits
Hands-on areas: water play, building, vet clinic, theater, rooftop playground
Special programs: Sensory Time, Community Access Nights, bilingual activities
Recent expansion with new play spaces
Earthquake adventure, wind area, toddler zones
attractions.tips: Children must be accompanied by adult 18+ at all times; no strollers on floors (carriers OK). Food only on 3rd floor. Late weekday afternoons least crowded. Sensory tools (earmuffs, sunglasses) available. Socks for rooftop. [FAQ](https://www.imaginecm.org/faq/)
attractions.nearby: Flying Squirrel Trampoline Park, Forest Park, Jetty Island, Evergreen Arboretum & Gardens, Howarth Park. [VisitEverett](https://www.visiteverett.com/148/Everett-with-Kids), [Tripadvisor](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g58466-Activities-zft11306-Everett_Washington.html)

attractions.name: Jetty Island
attractions.description: Jetty Island is a man-made island and park featuring the longest sandy beach in Puget Sound, with warm shallow waters ideal for swimming and playing. Accessible year-round by personal watercraft, it offers seasonal foot ferry service during summer months in partnership with the Port of Everett, City of Everett, and Snohomish County Parks. The island has no facilities, electricity, or running water, emphasizing its natural, untouched beauty.Snohomish County, Visit Everett, Wikipedia
attractions.address: Jetty Landing, 10th Street & West Marine View Drive, Port of Everett
attractions.hours: Ferry: Wed-Thu & Sun 10am-5:45pm; Fri-Sat 10am-6:45pm (departs every 15-30 min); reservations required. Island open year-round by boat.
attractions.days_open: Wed-Sun during Jetty Island Days (July 5 - Sunday of Labor Day weekend)
attractions.admission: Ferry: $3/person (Wed-Thu), $5/person (Fri-Sun) + taxes/fees; free for kids under 2 (reservation required); donations appreciated. Island free by personal boat.[Visit Everett FAQ](https://www.visiteverett.com/faq.aspx?TID=15)
highlights: Longest sandy beach (2 miles) in Puget Sound
Warm shallow waters perfect for families
Rich wildlife habitat and nature trails
Popular for kiteboarding and beach fires in designated pits
Seasonal ferry access with reservations
Year-round access by kayak/paddleboard/boat
attractions.tips: Reserve ferry tickets well in advance online as they sell out; bring food, water, shade, sunscreen, and pack out all trash—no concessions or facilities on island. Arrive 15 min early for boarding; grab return ticket upon arrival. Best on weekdays at low tide for warmer water and fewer crowds; watch for sinking mud from ghost shrimp burrows.[Visit Everett](https://www.visiteverett.com/1323/Your-Guide-to-Everetts-Jetty-Island), [FAQ](https://www.visiteverett.com/faq.aspx?TID=15)
attractions.nearby: Port of Everett Marina, Schack Art Center, Everett waterfront attractions.[ParentMap](https://www.parentmap.com/article/summer-fun-at-jetty-island)

attractions.name: Mukilteo Lighthouse Park
attractions.description: Mukilteo Lighthouse Park encompasses the historic 1906 lighthouse at the west end of Mukilteo, Washington, and 12 acres of waterfront parkland along Possession Sound. It features 1,500 feet of sandy beach, a public boat launch, shoreline walkway, playground, picnic areas, fire pits, and volleyball court. Formerly Mukilteo State Park, it was transferred to the City of Mukilteo in 2003 and offers stunning views of Whidbey Island across the water. The lighthouse is managed by the Mukilteo Historical Society and listed on the National Register of Historic Places.Wikipedia, Mukilteo Historical Society
attractions.hours: Park grounds: Dawn to dusk daily (approx. 6AM-8PM). Lighthouse, exhibits, gift shop: Saturdays and Sundays 12PM-5PM (or 1PM-4PM), April through September.[Mukilteo Historical Society](https://mukilteohistorical.org/visit/), [TripAdvisor](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g58625-d2357902-Reviews-Mukilteo_Lighthouse_Park-Mukilteo_Washington.html)
attractions.days_open: Park: Everyday. Lighthouse interior: Weekends April-September
attractions.admission: Free (donations appreciated for lighthouse). Paid parking: $2/hour (free for Mukilteo residents with pass)
highlights: Historic 1906 wooden lighthouse with operational Fresnel lens
1,500 ft sandy beach with fire pits and views of Whidbey Island
Public boat launch and ADA-accessible shoreline walkway
Playground, picnic tables, volleyball net
Mukilteo Light Station tours and gift shop (seasonal)
National Register of Historic Places listing
attractions.tips: Park for free with Mukilteo resident pass; otherwise budget for $2/hr parking. Bring firewood for beach fire pits (follow burn rules, no beach wood). Check seasonal lighthouse hours; grounds open year-round. Arrive early on weekends/ferry days for parking. Self-guided walking tour with QR codes available.
attractions.nearby: Mukilteo-Clinton Ferry Terminal (adjacent, to Whidbey Island), Future of Flight Aviation Center & Boeing Tour (3.5 miles), Japanese Gulch Trails (nearby hikes), Harbour Pointe Golf Club (5 miles)

attractions.name: North Cascades National Park
attractions.description: North Cascades National Park is an American national park in Washington state featuring the rugged North Cascades Range, the most expansive glacial system in the contiguous U.S., and diverse ecosystems from alpine meadows to old-growth forests. It is part of the North Cascades National Park Service Complex, which includes Ross Lake and Lake Chelan National Recreation Areas, offering hiking, boating, and wilderness experiences. The park spans over 500,000 acres with nearly 400 miles of trails and more glaciers than any other park outside Alaska.NPS, Wikipedia
attractions.hours: Visitor centers seasonal: North Cascades Visitor Center (Newhalem) 9am-5pm daily May 23-Sep 28; Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount) 7-11:30am & 12:30-4pm daily May 15-Oct 10, 2026; park roads/trails accessible year-round weather permitting.[NPS Hours](https://www.nps.gov/noca/planyourvisit/hours.htm)
attractions.days_open: Park open daily year-round; visitor centers seasonal (daily when open)
attractions.admission: Free[NPS Fees](https://www.nps.gov/noca/planyourvisit/fees.htm)
highlights: Most glaciers of any U.S. national park outside Alaska (300+)
Over 400 miles of hiking trails including Pacific Crest Trail sections
Stunning turquoise glacier-fed lakes like Diablo Lake
Rugged peaks like Goode Mountain (9,220 ft)
High biodiversity across 8 life zones
Scenic North Cascades Highway (SR 20) through the park
attractions.tips: Visit June-September for best access; check road conditions as SR 20 closes winter. Obtain backcountry permits for overnight stays. Bring bear spray, water filter, and layers for variable weather. Popular trails like Maple Pass Loop require early starts to avoid crowds. Northwest Forest Pass needed for some adjacent forest trailhead parking.[NPS](https://www.nps.gov/noca/planyourvisit/conditions.htm)
attractions.nearby: Ross Lake National Recreation Area, Lake Chelan National Recreation Area, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, North Cascades Highway scenic drive, Stehekin Valley.[NPS](https://www.nps.gov/noca/index.htm)

attractions.name: Skagit Valley Tulip Festival
attractions.description: Annual spring festival showcasing Washington's largest tulip gardens and fields in Skagit Valley near Mount Vernon. Features four main display gardens (RoozenGaarde, Garden Rosalyn, Tulip Valley Farms, Tulip Town) with vibrant blooms, free community events like parades and fairs, and scenic farmland views with Mt. Baker backdrop. Blooms are weather-dependent, typically late March to late April. tulipfestival.org, press release

[tulipfestival.org/garden-tickets](https://tulipfestival.org/garden-tickets/), [visitskagitvalley.com](https://www.visitskagitvalley.com/discover/skagit-valley-tulip-festival/)
attractions.days_open: Daily during bloom season (late March-April, weather-dependent)
attractions.admission: Festival free; gardens $15-23 adults (weekday/weekend varies), kids ~$10 or free under 3, seniors/military lower. Per garden. [tulipfestival.org/garden-tickets](https://tulipfestival.org/garden-tickets/), [seattleschild.com](https://www.seattleschild.com/events/skagit-valley-tulip-festival/)
highlights: 250+ acres of tulip fields
4 official gardens with 50+ varieties
Free events: parade, street fair, art shows
Dog-friendly options
Mt. Baker scenic backdrop
U-pick and night blooms
